title: Baghrir
subtitle: Moroccan semolina pancakes with a thousand holes
excerpt: Spongy semolina pancakes cooked on one side only, so the top surface is covered in small holes from the bubbles rising through the batter. The holes give the pancake its name and its texture: a lattice of pores that soaks up butter and honey. Served at breakfast with a drizzle of each.
sourceNotes: Baghrir (baghreer, mille trous) is the characteristic Moroccan semolina pancake. The holes form as carbon dioxide from the yeast and baking powder escapes upward through the thin batter. Baghrir is cooked only on one side, never flipped, the top surface with its holes is the serving side. It is a feature of the Moroccan breakfast table and Ramadan iftar.

content.text: The batter must be thin and pourable, similar to crepe batter. Blend until completely smooth, any lumps block the hole formation. The resting time after blending allows the yeast to start working and the batter to develop a slight fermented tang. Do not skip it.

Pour the batter into a cold, dry pan, then turn the heat to medium-low. Do not flip the baghrir. It cooks only on the bottom. The top surface should be dry and covered with holes by the time the bottom is set. If the holes do not form, the batter is too thick: add water a tablespoon at a time and re-test.

Combine the {{semolina}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-dried}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{eggs}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a blender. Add the {{water}} and blend for 1 minute until the batter is completely smooth. Leave to rest at room temperature for 30 minutes until slightly frothy on the surface. Heat a small non-stick pan (18 to 20 cm diameter) over medium-low heat. Pour a ladleful of batter into the cold or lightly heated pan to form a round about 15 cm across. Holes should begin forming within 1 to 2 minutes. Cook for 3 to 4 minutes until the surface is dry and the holes are set. Do not flip. Slide onto a plate. Repeat with the remaining batter. Serve drizzled with melted {{unsalted-butter}} and {{honey}}.

items.fix: Add warm water a tablespoon at a time and stir. The batter should be thinner than pancake batter. Also lower the heat, a high temperature sets the bottom too fast before bubbles can rise through
items.cause: Batter too thick, or pan too hot
items.symptom: No holes forming on the surface

items.fix: Use a non-stick pan. Allow it to warm briefly before adding batter, but not so hot that the batter sets on contact
items.cause: Pan not non-stick or pan too cool when batter added

items.fix: Blend for a full minute. The resting period allows the semolina to hydrate and soften. Very coarse semolina will always give a grainier result, use fine semolina
items.cause: Semolina not fully blended or batter not rested

content.text: Baghrir appears on the Moroccan breakfast table alongside msemen, mint tea, and honey. During Ramadan it is a staple of iftar, the meal that breaks the fast at sunset, eaten with butter and honey or with amlou. The holes that cover the surface are a visual indicator of a successful baghrir, a sign the yeast was active and the batter was right. In France and Britain it is sometimes made for weekend breakfasts as an alternative to crumpets, which the texture and the pore pattern closely resemble.
